title: Persist runner preference on bead and honour it during dispatch
description:
When an operator runs `takt --runner claude retry <bead_id>`, the intent is to run that specific bead with the Claude runner. Currently the runner choice is not stored anywhere on the bead, so if the scheduler is later invoked with a different default (e.g. via the TUI which uses the config default), the stored preference is ignored and the bead runs with the wrong runner.

## Changes required

### 1. Bead model (`src/agent_takt/models.py`)
Add an optional field `preferred_runner: str | None = None` to the `Bead` dataclass/model. This field holds the runner name (`"claude"`, `"codex"`) explicitly chosen by the operator, or `None` if no preference was set (use config default).

### 2. Retry command (`src/agent_takt/cli/commands/misc.py` or wherever `takt retry` is handled)
When `takt --runner <name> retry <bead_id>` is invoked, set `bead.preferred_runner = runner_name` before saving. When `takt retry` is invoked without `--runner`, leave `preferred_runner` as `None`.

### 3. Scheduler dispatch (`src/agent_takt/scheduler/core.py` or `execution.py`)
When dispatching a bead, if `bead.preferred_runner` is set, instantiate the runner for that backend instead of the scheduler-level default. After the bead completes (done or blocked), clear `preferred_runner` so it does not persist across retries unless explicitly set again.

### 4. CLI `bead show` output
Include `preferred_runner` in the JSON output when it is set, so operators can see which runner a bead is pinned to.

## Acceptance criteria
- `takt --runner claude retry B-xyz` sets `preferred_runner = "claude"` on the bead and the field is visible in `takt bead show B-xyz`
- When the scheduler dispatches a bead with `preferred_runner` set, it uses that runner regardless of the scheduler-level default
- When the scheduler dispatches a bead with `preferred_runner = None`, it uses the scheduler-level default (existing behaviour unchanged)
- After a bead completes or is blocked, `preferred_runner` is cleared
- Tests cover: retry sets preferred_runner; scheduler honours preferred_runner over default; preferred_runner cleared on completion
acceptance_criteria: preferred_runner field added to Bead model
takt --runner X retry sets preferred_runner on the bead
Scheduler uses preferred_runner over config default when set
preferred_runner cleared after bead completes or is blocked
takt bead show includes preferred_runner when set

handoff_summary.completed: Added `preferred_runner` to the bead model and round-trip serialization. `takt retry` now stores the explicit `--runner` choice on the bead and plain `retry` clears it back to `None`. Scheduler execution now resolves a per-bead runner override, uses that backend for isolated execution and dispatch, and clears the preference after terminal execution. `takt bead show` now includes `preferred_runner` only when set. Added unit coverage for model serialization, retry persistence, show output, runner override dispatch, default fallback, and clearing after completed/blocked runs.
handoff_summary.risks: Behavior depends on the existing backend names `codex` and `claude`; an invalid persisted value is treated as a blocked bead and the pin is cleared.
handoff_summary.conflict_risks: Runner selection and cleanup now span both `scheduler/execution.py` and `scheduler/finalize.py`; downstream changes touching scheduler dispatch or terminal-state persistence should preserve that lifecycle coupling.
handoff_summary.design_decisions: Resolved runner pinning inside `BeadExecutor` rather than `Scheduler` so the same backend choice drives both isolated-skill setup and the actual `run_bead()` call. Reused the existing scheduler runner when the backend already matches, and only instantiate a backend-specific runner when the bead pin differs. Cleared `preferred_runner` in finalization plus early blocked paths so the pin remains single-use across retries without leaking into later runs.
handoff_summary.test_coverage_notes: Tester should verify: `takt --runner claude retry <id>` persists `preferred_runner`, plain `takt retry <id>` clears it back to `None`, `takt bead show <id>` surfaces the field only when set, scheduler dispatch uses the pinned backend over the scheduler default, scheduler still uses the default backend when no pin exists, and the field is cleared after both completed and blocked executions. I only ran `uv run python -m compileall src tests` for syntax/import validation and did not run the test suite.
handoff_summary.known_limitations: Only the known backend names `codex` and `claude` are accepted at dispatch time; if storage somehow contains another value, the bead is blocked instead of attempting fallback.

conflict_risks: Runner selection and cleanup now span both `scheduler/execution.py` and `scheduler/finalize.py`; downstream changes touching scheduler dispatch or terminal-state persistence should preserve that lifecycle coupling.

execution_history.summary: Persisted per-bead runner preference, taught retry/show/scheduler paths to use it, and added coverage for serialization, CLI visibility, dispatch override, and post-run clearing.

guardrails.template_text:
# Developer Guardrails

Primary responsibility: Implement only the assigned bead inside the existing architecture and file scope.

Allowed actions:
- Modify code, configuration, or local docs required to complete the assigned bead.
- Create sub-beads for discovered follow-up work that should be handled separately.
- Verify your `{{LANGUAGE}}` changes compile and import correctly with a quick syntax check: `{{BUILD_CHECK_COMMAND}}`.

Disallowed actions:
- Run any test suite — not even a targeted `{{TEST_COMMAND}}` run or any subset. Test execution is exclusively the tester agent's responsibility. Running tests wastes the agent budget and delays the pipeline.
- Redesign unrelated architecture or broaden scope beyond the assigned bead.
- Perform final review signoff in place of a review agent.
- Silently absorb unrelated test, documentation, or planning work that should be handed off.

Required Handoff Fields:

Every developer bead **must** populate the following three fields in its output JSON. Reviewers and tester agents rely on these fields to scope their work without additional back-and-forth turns. Use `"N/A"` only when a field is genuinely inapplicable (e.g., a trivial rename with no design trade-offs); do not leave fields blank.

- **`design_decisions`** — Document non-obvious architectural or implementation choices made during this bead. Include alternatives considered and why the chosen approach was preferred. Reviewers use this to evaluate correctness without re-deriving intent. Set to `"N/A"` only if the change was purely mechanical with no meaningful trade-offs.

- **`test_coverage_notes`** — Describe what the tester agent should verify: new code paths, edge cases introduced, regression risks, and any known untested scenarios left to the tester. Set to `"N/A"` only if the bead touched no executable code paths (e.g., documentation-only changes).

- **`known_limitations`** — Call out constraints, deferred work, or known gaps in the implementation. This includes out-of-scope items deliberately left for follow-up beads and any areas where the implementation is intentionally incomplete. Set to `"N/A"` if the implementation is complete as specified with no known gaps.

Expected outputs:
- Completed or blocked JSON with concise implementation summary.
- Accurate touched files, changed files, risks, and follow-up handoff fields.
- Populated `design_decisions`, `test_coverage_notes`, and `known_limitations` fields in every response.
